one_line_takeaway: Introduces zero-knowledge 'succinct model difference proofs' letting an auditor verify -- without seeing model weights -- that a fine-tuned model's drift from a committed base model stays within a declared norm/rank/sparsity policy, with proof size/verify time provably independent of parameter count (demonstrated on real GPT-2 LoRA fine-tuning: 1.7MB proof, 10.5ms verify vs. 216MB raw diff), while proving a matching linear lower bound showing such succinctness is impossible without exploiting drift structure.
key_finding: Proof size and verification time for all three SMDP schemes are empirically constant across block dimensions from 1024^2 to 8192^2 (NBDP: 67 KB proof, 0.38-0.39 ms verify; MRDP: 2.2 KB proof, 0.02 ms verify; SDIP: 15 KB proof, 0.10 ms verify), while prover time scales roughly as O(dd') (quadrupling block dimension ~quadruples prover time), empirically confirming the succinctness claim is decoupled from parameter count. In the adversarial-detection experiment (500 trials/scenario on 4096x4096 blocks), MRDP and SDIP reject 100% of rank-inflation (rank-16 vs. declared rank-8) and sparsity-violation (k=500 vs. declared k=100) attacks respectively, consistent with their negligible (D/q < 2^-243) and q^-t soundness errors; NBDP rejects 498/500 (99.6%) of norm-overflow attacks (1.5*epsilon vs. declared epsilon), consistent with its statistical false-accept parameter delta=0.005. Against a generic Groth16 SNARK baseline for the norm check, Groth16 has a smaller proof (192 B vs. NBDP's 67 KB) and comparable verify time (3.2 ms) but a prover cost that is prohibitive at scale: ~42 minutes for a 1024^2 block and ~11 hours for a 4096^2 block, versus 1.2 s and 19.2 s respectively for NBDP. On the real GPT-2 LoRA end-to-end run, the aggregated FTI proof across 73 blocks is 1.7 MB (versus 216 MB for transmitting the raw parameter difference, a 127x reduction), verifies in 10.5 ms total, and the prover takes ~6.2 minutes on a single CPU core (claimed embarrassingly parallel across blocks, projected under 30 s with block-level parallelism, though this parallel figure is not itself measured). Singular-value analysis of the fine-tuned model confirms all 48 LoRA-modified attention blocks have effective rank exactly 8 and are accepted under a rank-8 policy, while tightening the policy to rank 4 causes rejection across all layers; non-LoRA layers show residual Frobenius drift between 0.3 and 2.9, within the declared epsilon=3.0 budget.
core_contribution: The paper formalizes 'fine-tuning integrity' (FTI): a cryptographic objective letting a verifier (auditor/regulator/platform) check, via zero-knowledge proofs, that a released fine-tuned model W* differs from a committed trusted base model W0 only within a policy-declared class of admissible parameter drift, without revealing either model's weights. It introduces Succinct Model Difference Proofs (SMDPs), a unified ZK-proof framework instantiated for three drift classes: NBDP (norm-bounded drift, via random Rademacher projections + range proofs, dimension-independent cost), MRDP (low-rank drift, via bivariate polynomial commitments and Schwartz-Zippel testing, cost scaling with rank r), and SDIP (sparse drift, via random linear checks over committed binary indicator vectors, cost scaling with sparsity k). For each construction the paper proves completeness, soundness, zero-knowledge, and succinctness, and additionally proves a matching Omega(n) communication lower bound (via reduction from the one-way promise Hamming-distance problem) showing that some structural assumption on the drift class is necessary for any non-interactive statistical proof system to be succinct. The framework is prototyped and evaluated on synthetic parameter blocks and a real LoRA fine-tune of GPT-2.
threat_model: Three-party setting: (1) a trusted provider publishes a base model W0 with a binding+hiding cryptographic commitment C0; (2) an untrusted fine-tuner, who has full white-box access to W0, produces an updated model W* and a proof of compliance with a verifier-specified drift policy P -- the fine-tuner may modify parameters arbitrarily (poisoned training data, adversarial fine-tuning, direct weight manipulation) and may adaptively construct proofs after seeing W*, with the goal of getting a policy-violating update accepted; (3) a verifier (regulator/auditor/platform operator) holds only (C0, C*, P, proof) and decides accept/reject without ever seeing raw weights. No trusted hardware/TEE is assumed. The paper is explicit that FTI enforces only structural compliance (e.g., 'this is a rank-8 update') and does NOT certify correctness, benign behavior, or the absence of backdoors/safety-alignment removal at the semantic level -- behavioral evaluation is stated as a necessary complement, not something the system provides. Motivating attacks named are backdoor injection, safety-alignment removal, and misrepresenting update structure (e.g., claiming rank-8 LoRA while actually shipping a higher-rank or larger-norm update).
limitations: The paper is explicit about several limitations in its own text: (1) FTI certifies only that a parameter update lies within a declared structural class -- it does not certify correctness or benign behavior of the resulting model, and cannot by itself detect backdoors or malicious behavior that stays within the declared drift budget; behavioral evaluation is stated as a necessary complement, not a substitute. (2) Under a Lipschitz assumption a Frobenius-norm bound on drift implies a bound on output change, but the paper notes this bound 'can be conservative,' i.e., a compliant norm-bounded update can still change model behavior substantially. (3) The drift policy P itself is set exogenously by the verifier/regulator and is not something the system infers or validates as 'safe' -- an inappropriate policy (e.g., too loose an epsilon or rank) still yields an 'accepted' certificate. (4) The prototype uses simulated cryptographic primitives (Merkle/SHA-256 commitments, simulated KZG interface) rather than an optimized production implementation, so absolute timing numbers understate what a real deployment would need engineering to achieve; the paper flags this itself and only claims to validate scaling behavior, not production performance. (5) The KZG-style commitments used in MRDP require a trusted setup; the paper notes STARK- or inner-product-argument-based alternatives could remove this at the cost of larger proofs, but does not evaluate them. (6) The lower bound theorem covers only non-interactive, statistical (information-theoretic) proof systems for unstructured (Hamming-ball) drift with constant soundness/completeness error under a stated t<n/4 regime, and does not rule out succinct proofs for other unstructured drift models or computationally-sound (rather than statistical) constructions. (7) The reviewer notes that end-to-end GPT-2 parallel-prover claim ('under 30 seconds with block-level parallelism') is stated as a projection, not a measured result.

datasets_used: WikiText-103 (used to LoRA fine-tune GPT-2 for the end-to-end evaluation)
Synthetic parameter blocks of size 1024x1024, 2048x2048, 4096x4096, 8192x8192 with injected norm-bounded/rank-8/k=100-sparse drift
quantitative_results: NBDP (m=40 projections): 67 KB proof size and 0.38-0.39 ms verification time, constant across block sizes 1024^2 to 8192^2; prover time 1.2s (1024^2) to 78.5s (8192^2)
MRDP (r=8): 2.2 KB proof size and 0.02 ms verification time, constant across block sizes; prover time 1.8s (1024^2) to 115.2s (8192^2)
SDIP (k=100): 15 KB proof size and 0.10 ms verification time, constant across block sizes; prover time 3.1s (1024^2) to 198.7s (8192^2)

Adversarial detection (500 trials/scenario, 4096x4096 blocks): MRDP rejects rank-16-vs-policy-rank-8 attack 500/500 (100%); SDIP rejects k=500-vs-policy-k=100 attack 500/500 (100%); NBDP rejects 1.5eps-vs-policy-eps attack 498/500 (99.6%)
Groth16 baseline (norm circuit) vs. NBDP on 1024^2 block: Groth16 proof 192 B / verify 3.2 ms / prover ~42 min, vs. NBDP proof 67 KB / verify 0.38 ms / prover 1.2 s
Groth16 baseline on 4096^2 block: proof 192 B / verify 3.2 ms / prover ~11 hr, vs. NBDP proof 67 KB / verify 0.39 ms / prover 19.2 s

GPT-2 (124M params) end-to-end FTI certification: aggregated proof 1.7 MB across 73 blocks vs. 216 MB raw parameter difference (127x reduction); total verification 10.5 ms; total prover time ~6.2 minutes on a single CPU core (projected, not measured, under 30s with block-level parallelism)

GPT-2 breakdown: Attention (MRDP, r=8, 48 blocks): 106 KB proof, 0.96 ms verify, 1.4 min prover. Feedforward (NBDP, m=40, 24 blocks): 1.6 MB proof, 9.4 ms verify, 4.6 min prover. Embedding (SDIP, k=200, 1 block): 30 KB proof, 0.10 ms verify, 12.4 s prover
MRDP soundness error bound D/q < 2^-243 for d,d' <= 4096 and 256-bit prime field q

All 48 LoRA-modified attention blocks in GPT-2 confirmed to have effective rank exactly 8 via singular value analysis and accepted under rank-8 policy; rejected under a tightened rank-4 policy; non-LoRA layers show Frobenius drift between 0.3 and 2.9, within declared epsilon=3.0
baselines_compared: Generic zk-SNARK (Groth16) encoding the norm-bounded check as an arithmetic/R1CS circuit -- used only as a cost baseline for the norm-bounded (NBDP) case, not for rank or sparsity
Raw full-parameter-difference transmission (216 MB, discloses weights, no structural guarantee)
Hash-based integrity check (32 bytes, provides no structural drift-class guarantee, cited only qualitatively, not benchmarked)
